summary:
The Great Famine in Ireland (An Gorta Mór, 'the Great Hunger'), which ravaged the country from 1845 to 1852, was the worst demographic catastrophe in nineteenth-century Europe. Triggered by a potato blight caused by the water mold Phytophthora infestans that destroyed the potato crop — the primary food source for roughly one-third of Ireland's population — the famine killed approximately one million people and drove another million to emigrate in its first years alone.

The scale of suffering was devastating. Ireland's population, which stood at approximately 8 million in 1841, was reduced to 6.5 million by 1851 and continued to fall through emigration for the rest of the century. In the worst-affected western counties, where poverty and dependence on the potato were greatest, entire communities were annihilated. Workhouses designed to accommodate the destitute were overwhelmed. People died by the roadside. Coffin ships — overcrowded emigrant vessels with appallingly high mortality rates — carried hundreds of thousands to the United States, Canada, Australia, and Britain.

The British government's response remains deeply controversial. The Whig administration under Lord Russell adhered rigidly to laissez-faire economic doctrine and the principle that private markets should handle food distribution, even as food was exported from Ireland throughout the famine. The cessation of public works programs and relief schemes at critical moments, the inadequacy of soup kitchen provisions, and the enforcement of rent evictions that drove hundreds of thousands from their homes created a widespread Irish conviction that British policy amounted to genocide or at least criminal negligence.

The famine's legacy was transformative and long-lasting. It created a vast Irish diaspora — by 1900 more people of Irish descent lived outside Ireland than within it — and implanted a deep and enduring grievance against British rule that fueled Irish nationalism for generations. The population of Ireland has never returned to its pre-famine level. In Irish memory, the famine stands as the central trauma of modern Irish history, shaping political identity and the relationship with Britain well into the twentieth and twenty-first centuries.
